violations.explanation: Locator access is missing .elements. namespace. The framework pattern requires accessing locators via page_obj.elements.locator_name when using the Elements inner class pattern. The page_additions file defines plan_pause_info as a selector string in the Elements class, so it must be accessed via print_history_page.elements.plan_pause_info.
Locator access is missing .elements. namespace. Same issue as V001 - must use .elements. to access the locator defined in the Elements inner class.
Locator access is missing .elements. namespace. The billing_cycle_period_card locator is defined in the Elements class and must be accessed via .elements. namespace.

violations.explanation: Page additions file does not follow the Elements inner class pattern. Locators are defined as standalone module-level variables instead of being inside an Elements class. The framework requires locators to be defined as class attributes within an Elements inner class so they can be accessed via page_obj.elements.locator_name pattern. The entire file structure is incorrect - it's written as a documentation file with instructions rather than valid Python code.

fix_details.description: Complete file restructure required. The file must be rewritten as valid Python code with proper Elements class structure.
fix_details.before_structure: Standalone variable assignments with documentation comments

violations.explanation: Module-level docstring is excessively verbose with detailed merge instructions that should not be in production code. The docstring should be concise and focus on what the file contains, not how to merge it. Detailed merge instructions belong in documentation or comments, not in the module docstring.

"""Additional locators for PrintHistoryPage.

This file contains new locators for the Billing Cycle Period card functionality
to be merged into pages/print_history_page.py.

Target File: pages/print_history_page.py
Test Case: C44873414 - Billing Cycle Period card

This file contains additions for pages/print_history_page.py in TWO parts:

PART 1 - SELECTOR STRINGS FOR Elements INNER CLASS:
Open pages/print_history_page.py and locate the Elements inner class (defined inside PrintHistoryPage class).
Add the selector string definitions from the 'Elements Class Additions' section below as class attributes.
These are simple string assignments at the class level.

PART 2 - PROPERTY METHODS FOR PrintHistoryPage CLASS:
In the same file, locate the PrintHistoryPage class body (the main class, NOT the Elements inner class).
Add the property methods from the 'Property Methods' section below at the same indentation level as other class methods.
These provide convenient access to locators via print_history_page.property_name pattern.

VERIFICATION:
- Elements inner class should contain string selectors as class attributes (e.g., plan_pause_info = "[data-testid='plan-pause-info']")
- PrintHistoryPage class body should contain @property methods that return self.page.locator(self.elements.selector_name)
- Test scripts access via: print_history_page.property_name (e.g., print_history_page.plan_pause_info)
- Update tooltip selectors if actual data-testid attributes differ
- Run test to verify all locators work correctly

MERGE NOTES:
- Tooltip selectors use :near() pseudo-selector to distinguish between complimentary and additional pages tooltips
- Test tooltip interactions on both desktop (hover) and mobile (click)
- NOTE: total_printed_pages may already exist in PrintHistoryPage - verify before merging to avoid duplicates
"""

violations.explanation: File contains instructional comments and section headers that make it invalid Python for direct import. The file is structured as a documentation/instruction file rather than executable Python code. Comments like '# ADD THESE SELECTOR STRINGS TO...' and section dividers with equals signs are not appropriate for production code. The file cannot be imported or executed as-is.

fix_details.description: Remove all instructional comments and section dividers. Structure as valid Python with proper class definitions.
fix_details.before_structure: Instructional comments + standalone assignments + property methods with incorrect indentation
fix_details.after_structure: Valid Python with Elements class containing selectors and properly indented property methods

violations.explanation: Property methods have incorrect indentation. They are indented as if they are inside a class, but they are at module level in this file. Since this is a page additions file meant to be merged, the property methods should be at module level (no indentation) with a comment indicating they should be added to the PrintHistoryPage class body. The current indentation makes them invalid Python at module level.

fix_priority_order.fix_approach: Restructure the page additions file to follow proper Elements inner class pattern. Remove all instructional comments and section dividers. Create a proper Elements class with selector strings as class attributes. Ensure property methods are at module level with no indentation and include comments indicating they should be merged into PrintHistoryPage class body.

Add .elements. namespace to all locator accesses in the test script. Replace print_history_page.locator_name with print_history_page.elements.locator_name for all locators defined in the Elements class (plan_pause_info, billing_cycle_period_card, etc.).
Remove all instructional comments, section headers with equals signs, and 'ADD THESE...' style comments. The file should contain only valid Python code with standard docstrings and inline comments.
Ensure import order follows framework standards: stdlib → third-party → core → pages → helpers → common. The common import should be last, after all helper imports, separated by a blank line.
Simplify module docstring to be concise. Remove detailed merge instructions from docstring - keep only essential information about target file and test case.
Remove redundant inline comment '# Pause subscription via Rails Admin' from precondition section. The section comment and log statement already provide sufficient context.
